speaker: interviewer
text: Faigie Libman. Faigie, in the last while, we were talking about Stutthof and the satellite camps that sprung up in that area.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell Stutthof?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the camps that you were at for us, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stutthof, S-T-U-T-T-H-O-F. That was a concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what country?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think Germany. OK. Now, the satellite camps that we went to the labor camps to work were Trunz, T-R-U-N-Z, and Merzen, M-E-R-Z-E-N, and Lubicz, L-U-B-I-C-Z. Those were the labor camps that we worked digging ditches. And like I said before, if our situation in the ghetto was bad, this was terrible. At least in the ghetto, we slept in a house. Here, we slept on the cold, cold ground, only straw in tents. And somehow, by miracle, by what, we survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you out to work each day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Most of the day. Most of the day.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you march from the camps to the place that you worked?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we were like-- it was-- it wasn't-- it wasn't far away. And somehow we survived. It was the fittest who did. If you got very sick, that was your end. All right, my mother was a nurse there. She gave first aid, but-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and the Germans, the Nazis made sure if you were-- they saw that you were weak, they saw that you were not capable, again, out of the word [NON-ENGLISH], you were finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work every day?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so, yes. It's like it's a blur a little bit regarding the days. Like they roll-- all roll into one, because there was nothing else. You didn't even-- in the ghetto, at least, sometimes we hid and we sing, and we had-- in the first labor camp, with my father, there was a concert. This was nothing. This was just work and suffering.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there daily appels at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Daily what?

speaker: interviewer
text: Appel. Roll call.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure. Oh, yes. So definitely. In Stutthof also after we got there, daily, daily, daily. They were very meticulous. And that's the way life went on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there-- were there ever any incidents that you witnessed where people were missing at the appel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened? What was the result?

speaker: interviewee
text: They just disappeared. They didn't come up--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any punishments meted out?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I remember more punishments like in the ghetto, more.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in the labor camps, at what time of day were you given your food rations?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think during the middle of the day.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you got back to the barracks or the tents--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were hungry.

speaker: interviewer
text: --at night.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were hungry. Sometimes they gave us something, but very little. It was all a-- a watery soup it was. I don't know. It boggles my mind, to tell you the truth. Sometimes I feel that I'm like an out of body experience, that it really didn't happen to me. Because no vitamins, no food, no fruit. How did we make it?

speaker: interviewer
text: I can't to this day-- I'm a little bit older now. I can't-- I think it didn't happen to me. It couldn't. How did we survive all that? See, in the ghetto, we still had somebody-- my mother snuck in, or you shared. This was nothing. This we lived on practically nothing. How did we make it? I have no answer, because I don't know. I suppose the sheer will to live. Because now, if you're not having a fruit, or if you're not having a vitamin, and you're a child, you fall apart. And here, I survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not everybody did. Some women were bloated from hunger. But as the war came closer for our liberation, we didn't know. We woke up one day-- that I remember-- and we were told we were marching.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we get to that, I wanted to ask you, at night, how-- how big were the tents that you were housed in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people per tent?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some were smaller, some were bigger. I think about [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 40, 50 to start with. Then smaller ones. As we were moving on, they were smaller.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at night, after the strenuous work, you lay down.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody fell asleep, dead to the world.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was no conversation.

speaker: interviewee
text: You didn't-- you didn't-- the young ones. I-- with my friend Gitel. I was always with her, encouraging her.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you talk about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Talk about what is going-- what we're going to do when we're free. I still believed. I-- I suppose my saving grace was that I had a very big, positive belief. I'm a terrible optimist to this day. Sometimes it gets me into trouble. I--

speaker: interviewer
text: You talk about your childhood with one another, swapping stories.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. And telling stories, and telling-- she was a little bit older, a couple of years, so I was sort of in awe of her. Very good-looking.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she was Lithuanian as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but from a smaller town. Very good-looking.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you all speak among one another?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yiddish, Yiddish, Yiddish. Yiddish. Yiddish. Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Hungarians--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Hungarians spoke more Hungarian. They mostly-- I don't remember hearing them talk in Yiddish, only like to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was communication between the various communities.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there was. Not as much, but there was. See, everybody wanted to-- to-- to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did that express itself in daily behavior? Can you describe any--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, everybody, we-- everybody in the lab-- everybody was nice to each other. Everybody tried to help each other. That I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there men in these labor camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no, it couldn't be. Remember, the men were separated. They were taken away. No men at all. Stutthof had mostly women.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, the Germans that were present in these camps, were they quartered separately?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They were quartered separately, sure. In-- in-- they had huts. I don't know what they had. They were not together. Or maybe their tents were very well-equipped. They were doing very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there only men, or were there women among them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only men. That I remember, only men.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever hear of any incidents where these Nazis were using inmates for sexual purposes?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was too young. I don't remember anything of that. Too young and too naive, I suppose. I wasn't exposed. Maybe. I don't know. I'm sure in the concentration camps, things like that were going on. But the labor camps, I don't know. I don't-- I can't recall anything. We only wanted to survive. And then one day, they came and told us that we are moving on.

speaker: interviewer
text: What day was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in the winter. It must have been [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the beginning of January, the new year. The beginning of January.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1945. That we're moving on. It must've been the middle of January. And we were moving and moving, and we were-- we used to march during the day, find a place. They used to find us a place anywhere near the road, in abandoned huts, and they kept on pushing and pushing and pushing that the people should march. And again, people were left on the side of the road. I mean, it's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any idea how many of you left Lubicz?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. Quite a few. But by the end, there were not. Very few. And we were marching and marching and marching, again, slept at-- slept at night, and marching and marching, and one day--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did this march continue for?

speaker: interviewee
text: It had to be at least a week or so, because we--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you receive any rations during that period?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very little. Very little. It had to be, or unless we started late in the middle of January, because I remember the date when we were liberated. That I will remember. And we were one-- that was-- we were marched, and we were-- everybody was exhausted. We got to abandoned cabins or something, and they were supposed to set it on fire. They were supposed to kill us. I guess they didn't have enough time-- or dynamite us. When we woke up in the morning, there were no soldiers. There was nobody there.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what direction from Stutthof were you marching?

speaker: interviewee
text: Towards Germany. Towards-- don't forget.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any particular city that you recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't. Towards Danzig. Towards civilization, to away from the Allies. The Allies were pressing. The Russians were pressing on. This is why we were marched.

speaker: interviewer
text: When they said we're moving on, did they give any reasons for it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like earlier, they said we're relocating you to new homes. This time--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. Then it was already-- I think our guards were already jittery also.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see any change in their attitude towards you as the war came to an end?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, maybe a little bit nicer, but it was already too late, because they had instructions to kill us. And they disobeyed. They left in the middle of the night, because when we woke up, there was nobody there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What date was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was January the 22nd, 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: You awoke in the morning?

speaker: interviewee
text: We woke up in the morning, and there was nobody. It was deathly quiet. And we went-- we were afraid to venture out because in case there's dynamite. You know, who knew what was happening? And we saw-- the first thing, a soldier on a white horse, a Russian soldier coming towards us.

speaker: interviewer
text: You knew he was Russian from his uniform?

speaker: interviewee
text: From his uniform and from that star, the red star on his hat. You know those gray hats? And he also told us that he was Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he spoke in what language to you? Russian?

speaker: interviewee
text: Russian. Russian. And don't forget, a lot of us spoke Russian. And right away, they grabbed him, and they pulled him down from the-- from the horse. And the women started to kiss him. A lot of-- and my-- I remember my mother. He brought food, and I remember my mother yelling at the people not to gorge, not to eat. Because after not eating, quite a few people died. I remember my mother telling people not to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall the name of this Russian liberating soldier?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That was-- I made a mistake. I should have remembered it. But it was-- we were in a turmoil. We like-- we didn't-- we were half dead. Even the-- the healthy ones were half dead. They gave us food. They took us to a hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russian. Russian-- Russian soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: But where were you taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in backwards. They marched us backwards, not towards, backwards. And they marched us towards-- their objective was to take us to Litha, to Kaunas. Take us to Lithuania. We all wanted to go, those who are from Lithuania extraction, because the first thing you wanted to know, who else was liberated?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that feeling when you realized you woke up that morning, 22nd of January, and it's all over?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was like-- it's all over. It was exhilarating, and yet who else-- what about my father? That was my main concern. What about my father? And when can I get some food?

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother? What was her reaction?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her reaction was probably the same thing. That we survived, and that we survive and go and find more of our relatives. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was our main objective. And we started to look. We used to-- don't forget, the war wasn't finished yet officially, so everything was in a turmoil. We went back, backwards, and we used to hop on cars to get faster, and trains. And I remember an incident when my-- my mother wanted I should get off. She got off first, and she yelled to me, schnell, schnell. Schnell, schnell in Yiddish is fast, fast. Schnell, schnell is in German too. We were arrested. They thought we were Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who arrested you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Lithuanian-- the-- the-- the Russians. They arrest, because there was-- it was under Russian administration. Everything, the Russian allies were in that part of the country.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it take to clear up any misunderstanding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very fast. Very fast, because my mother spoke Russian. See, my mother spoke Russian. And I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she-- did any of you have any form of identification with you at that stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. So we were traveling from place to place to place to place to look for relatives. If there was any-- when we found out anywhere that there was a list of survivors, and we were trying to get to Lithuania, because we thought my father would go there too. Now, we met a friend of my father's, and told us that my father was alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was on the way. On the way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in Poland, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: We must have been in Poland. He told us my father was alive. It was Poland. My father was alive. Now, so my mother had an idea not to go back to Lithuania. And because we were told on the American side, they work faster. You'll be able-- in Lodz, there are documents, there are papers on the walls who survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now came the worst time, because we didn't want to go to Lithuania, because we had-- we were in a camp with the Russians. They were treating us very nicely. What do we do to run away?

speaker: interviewee
text: It took a long time, and I suppose a lot of courage to travel at night and get up again on trains as displaced persons to cross the border, the Russian border, into the-- into the American side. You know, they had American sides, the Russian side, to-- into the Allied side.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were you with?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother. Just my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: The two of you.

speaker: interviewee
text: We latched on some other people were too that wanted, but it had to be on a very QT. It had to be the quietest thing, because we wanted to get to the American side to see who survived. We knew that my mother's family, that the small towns were wiped out. That we knew right away. But we had hope, because my father's friend said, we had hope. My father being very strong, will survive. If we survived, he survived. So we went from place to place until we decided to go, as I said before, on the American side.

speaker: interviewer
text: Name some of the places that you were at along the way.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. All Polish-- Polish places. I remember we crossed the border, and we got into Lodz. That I remember, getting into Lodz, because that was-- again, I can't-- you can't measure your life being in a traumatic situation living through what I went through. But I guess that was the worst time-- when I went to Lodz, and I found out that my father didn't make it. I think, like to say now in this vernacular, I lost my cool. I went completely berserk. And there wasn't any counselors or anybody to give a hand. I suppose, again, my will to live.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was his name on a list?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was on a list. And we also met more friends, and they told us in detail that he gave up completely. That's when we found out. He didn't want to live.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he died where?

speaker: interviewee
text: He died in Dachau. When they were also marching out of Dachau, he died in 22 days in Tavid. It came out in January of 1945. Can you imagine the irony of all this? He died in the same month that we were liberated. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't know the exact month. Years later in Montreal, it was-- I found out that he died on the way somewhere in 22 days in Tavid. And again, I had my mother. I suppose, life goes on. And we tried to get into a DP camp. And that must have been the time already when they put-- we got to Bad Gastein, and we got to Bad Gastein in November in 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you spent a large part of 1945.

speaker: interviewee
text: A large-- wandering. Wandering around. Wandering around like the wandering Jew. Wandering around trying to find-- that was the objective of mostly everybody, to find relatives. Maybe, maybe, maybe one person-- my mother had a big family. OK, my father's family emigrated, but my mother had a big family. Where was-- there was nobody left. And my aunt's sons were grown. They were grown. My mother was the [NON-ENGLISH]. It means she was the youngest one. So maybe one survived. They had come to Russia, had five sons, and [INAUDIBLE] had also, had four children. She was, I think, next to my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in Lodz you discovered the death of all the family members.

speaker: interviewee
text: That everybody that was-- like we were-- we thought-- we-- we found out going towards Lithuania, going towards Kaunas. But that for sure, we found out in Lodz that nobody survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go back to Kaunas?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Once you discovered--

speaker: interviewee
text: Upon discovered, no. She wouldn't-- my mother wouldn't hear of it. We wanted to leave, leave Europe, leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wandering through Poland during this 10, 11-month period, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there were other people milling around, chaos.

speaker: interviewee
text: Chaos.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe that a little bit for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Chaos, chaos, chaos. We didn't know where we were going to sleep. We didn't know when-- where we were going to eat. But people stuck together. You met people. This one gave you an idea, this one gave you an idea. And then we also wanted to go out of the Russian sector, you see. So that was-- pushed us on to go to-- and then we found that there was the displaced persons camp. DP camps in Bad Gastein, Austria, was a very nice place.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wanted to ask you, before we get to there in a minute, wandering through Poland as a survivor, did you come across local population, non-Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was their response?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very anti-Semitic, especially the Lith-- especially the Lithuanians. Very anti-Semitic. They killed quite a few people after the war. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm sorry that I have to mention, but it is the truth. The Lithuanians were very big anti-- anti-Semites, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: And the Polish weren't?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Polish also. The Ukrainian also. But the Lithuanians, in my experience, were the most.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did-- who-- who put you in contact with this camp? How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: The administration, Jewish administration in Lodz. Don't forget, there had to be-- somebody took charge. Somebody had to be. They wanted to save [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], the rest of the-- the remnants of-- of-- of the-- of the war. They had to save, so there were-- there were like-- people were helping-- helping each other a great deal to-- to-- to-- to come to something. And when-- and we went to Bad Gastein, which again, my mother became a nurse again in the infirmary, and I met a lady. In fact, I met a lady at the 50th anniversary. We were with her in Bad Gastein, but she tells the story at a 50th. Her name is Eisner. She is also speaking a lot. We met her, I-- and she told me a story that I didn't remember. She was very pregnant in Bad Gastein. My mother was the nurse in the infirmary. The doctor that looked after her was a big, burly German. She was deathly afraid of her, and she claims my mother saved her son and saved her life. My mother stood up to this doctor and said-- she had terrible labor-- labor pains. Terrible, she was very bad. And my mother pushed him away and said, I will look after this woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of this doctor at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't remember. She would, but she says because three weeks later, he was indicted of killing Jewish children. That same doctor, this German doctor, this Nazi. So she told me on the 50th anniversary, when we met for the weekend of celebrating, because she-- we were together in Bad Gastein.

speaker: interviewee
text: Her brother was my very good friend. Because don't forget, when we got in Bad Gastein, [NON-ENGLISH]. We all latched-- everybody latched onto somebody else to have a-- a continuity, you know? So everybody became such good friends. Families were lost.

speaker: interviewee
text: Families were lost. Because as I got there in 1945, until '47, we made fantastic friends. And she was there, and her-- she had a husband, and she had a brother, and the husband's brother. We became very-- they married. We became very close friends, the two boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a school set up in Bad Gastein?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Right away, immediately. And we started again about Yiddish [YIDDISH], about the holidays, and Hebrew a little bit. In fact, a Mr. [NON-ENGLISH] was my teacher that was the teacher that associated. He's retired. So when I started an associate, and I saw him, because I went to Montreal, and he went to Toronto.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were in Bad Gastein?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bad Gastein, he was our teacher. We met a lot of people. I made an awful lot of friends in Bad Gastein.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you remain in Bad Gastein?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until 1947, and then we were transferred to Ebelsberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Maybe they wanted to renovate, because Bad Gastein is a resort town with skiing, with-- with-- it's a-- it's a gorgeous place. We used to go-- we made makeshift skis, and we used to--

speaker: interviewer
text: What country were these DP camps in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Austria. Ebelsberg was right near Salzburg, so Bad Gastein was a gorgeous place with mountains, with snow. We recuperated our strength in Bad Gastein.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was a nurse. That was her life.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know that, but--

speaker: interviewee
text: And she also--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did she recuperate? How was her spirits after this experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the experience, she had some friends also. Like, she made friends, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you-- she made a decision she wanted to leave Europe.

speaker: interviewee
text: She made a decision she wanted to leave Europe. So who was left? Her brother in South Africa, her sister in Montreal, and her sister in New Rochelle, New York, in the United States. Papers were filled out-- because don't forget, the Germans are very meticulous. It was-- that was-- they-- all the papers were there, like after Stutthof, I'm talking about. And we started to make out papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who supervised these camps, Bad Gastein and Ebelsberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think under [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the UNRRA. The UNRRA, and they were Jews, and they were Americans. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we filled out papers, and it took until 1948 of March, the end of March, the 21st of March, that we landed in Halifax.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wanted to ask you, your-- in these DP camps in Austria--

speaker: interviewee
text: I made a lot--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any contact with Austrian non-Jewish people at any time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. Not-- not much. I didn't. I had friends among the Jews. I had my first boyfriend in camp. He was very cute, and it's a very cute story how he became-- how he was my boyfriend, how he liked me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- I was my mother's--

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name, first of all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rakhmiel I don't-- what was it-- Lazofsky. Look how I remember. Rakhmiel Lazofsky. I liked his brother better. His brother, Philip, was older, Fievel. I liked his brother better. But he must have liked me, but I was my mother's pride and joy. Once coming out of the-- we all ate together, you know, in the dining room. Rakhmiel threw snowballs at me, and I went running to my mother. I don't know whether a snowball hit me in the head or whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you touched my mother's child. She went to him, and he didn't-- his mother died during the war. He was only him and his brother. No more?
